data.title: Upstart: Bank Charter Is The Future
data.description:
Upstart Holdings, Inc. (UPST) was poised to be a big AI winner, but the company has failed to fully re-launch the AI lending marketplace the last couple of years. The company has faced artificial pain with a reliance on investing partners in order to fund loans no matter how great the loan performance. My investment thesis is far more Bullish on the stock dip to the prior lows around $20 while the company pursues a promising bank charter.

Bank Charter Opportunity

Since online lending platforms started in at least the 2010s, the biggest issue has been the loss of funding sources during economic weakness. Upstart felt this issue back in 2022 and the business has never fully recovered despite expansion into other lending segments beyond personal loans.

Both LendingClub (LC) and SoFi (SOFI) obtained banking charters to solve a big part of the issues with reliance on outside funding sources. LendingClub started with the same original plan of Upstart to rely on fees of originating and/or servicing personal loans issued on the platform, but the fintech eventually realized no matter how successful the loan performance, lending partners had other goals and risk tolerance.

The goal of generating fee income without taking credit risk was attractive, but the business is hostage to other financial partners. If the loans are actually performing strong, the fintech should just hold the loans and participate in the profits from the interest income.

For this reason, the biggest negative on Upstart was the lack of investing in loans when external loan funds dried up. On March 10, the fintech announced plans to pursue a national bank charter, though the company appeared to back away from truly expanding into lending to consumers directly using customer deposits.

In the press release, President and Chief Capital Officer Sanjay Datta had the following to say about the bank charter:

Banks, credit unions, and institutional funds will continue to be the capital source for the vast majority of all loans originated on the Upstart platform. We are not seeking to compete with our depository partners for local customer deposits and checking accounts.

Upstart appears to suggest part of the goal is a streamlined regulatory framework. The company suggests the Upstart Bank can access deposit funding, but the company isn't going to compete with banking partners and doesn't place much emphasis on investing in loans originated on the platform.

As of the end of 2025, Upstart had nearly $1 billion invested in loans held by the bank. The company invests primarily in R&D loans in new products like the ramping business in auto and home originations, but management has always mentioned a goal of reducing the loan balances.

For Q4'25, the fintech had $61 million in interest income with $31 million in net income after interest expense and fair value adjustments. The guidance for 2026 was only net interest income of ~$100 million, so the current business is minimal.

Fallen Very Far

The stock has basically traded at these lows since back in 2022. Upstart saw quarterly revenue reach $300 million back at the end of 2021 and ended 2025 with revenues back to the prior high.

The fintech now forecasts 2026 revenues of only $1.4 billion and forecasts a 35% revenue growth rate from 2025 through 2028. The crazy part is that Upstart traded up to nearly $400 back in late 2021 and the fintech is ready to roll past the prior peak levels while the stock is at only $25 now.

Upstart doesn't even trade at 2x sales now and the stock is even cheaper with an EBITDA goal of nearly $300 million. Upstart trades at less than 10x adjusted EBITDA estimates with a market cap at $2.5 billion.

The outlook has a terminal EBITDA margin target of 25% with a revenue goal amounting to nearly $2.5 billion. The stock only trades at 1x this revenue target while the terminal EBITDA goal is $625 million, or just 4x the market cap.

Note, the financial targets were provided before Upstart announced plans to launch a national bank. If the company was to become more aggressive with funding loans via deposits, the AI lending marketplace could grow revenues at a faster clip and reduce risk of losing funding partners. Also, a higher focus on interest income reduces revenue volatility.

Since the banking charter will take time to obtain regulatory approval, Upstart is highly reliant on banking partners and private credit investors. The company just announced a forward flow agreement for $1 billion, but Upstart could ultimately face some pressure as private credit investors face redemptions.

Ultimately, the biggest risk is a weak economic period and rising credit losses. If the AI lending marketplace doesn't produce superior returns for a period, Upstart will lose institutional and bank partner funds.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Upstart is far too cheap here. The AI lending marketplace has a potential strong catalyst with a future bank charter while the stock is back to the lows. Either way Upstart appears set to report record revenues and profits in 2026 and the stock shouldn't trade at the lows.

data.title: Amazon: Short-Term Pressure, Long-Term Opportunity
data.description:
Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) has seen a small dip in its share price, recently prompting me to write this article. Amazon’s fundamentals are very strong: In FY25, Amazon's revenue grew by 12% to $716.9 billion, operating income to roughly $80.0B, and AWS revenue to $128.7B. In Q4 2025, AWS grew 24%, its fastest growth in 13 quarters, while advertising grew 22%, according to management. Andy Jassy, CEO of Amazon, also strongly believes that AWS can reach $600 billion in annual revenue by 2036. AWS is also the world's largest cloud provider, taking up about 28-30% of market share. For these reasons and others that I will cover, I rate AMZN as a Strong Buy.

This doesn’t go without risk. Amazon is in one of the most competitive industries in the world right now. Although they are the number 1 cloud provider, Microsoft (MSFT) Azure and Alphabet's (GOOG) Google Cloud are creeping up quickly. There is also a risk that Amazon is overspending on AI, and their free cash flows may remain depressed and crunch their return on invested capital. More of this below.

Financial Overview

In FY25, Amazon performed very well. Total revenue was $716.9B, up from $638.0B in FY24. Operating income and net income also saw growth in FY25, now roughly $80.0B and $77.7B roughly. Even more notably, operating and net margins are approximately 11.2% and 10.8% in FY25, up from 10.8% and 9.3% in FY24.

Let’s get into the main reasons why Amazon has seen a strong FY25 performance. Amazon’s biggest driver remains to be AWS, up 20% YoY and 24% in Q4 2025. Driven by cloud demand, AI workloads, and enterprise adoption. This is Amazon’s growth engine, and I believe this is just the beginning for AWS’ expansion because of their AI investments.

In FY25, Amazon’s advertising is another high-growth, high-margin segment, being 22%, says management. This explains that there are more sellers on Amazon and higher monetization per user. Amazon’s retail business is more modest in comparison to AWS and advertising but has still seen double-digit growth. North American and International retail have both grown by 10% and 13% in FY25, driven by faster deliveries, more prime usage, and lower pricing compared to competitors.

In the chart above, we can see the main concern is their free cash flow (FCF). Although Amazon’s cash flow from operations has grown to $139.5B in Q4 2025, Amazon’s capex has risen dramatically higher. Their PP&E spending was $131.9B, offset by $3.5B in proceeds and incentives, making net PP&E $128.3B. Operating profitability improved greatly, but FCF clearly did not. However, this is not a stress point for me as an investor. Amazon is taking calculated, deliberate, and prudent bets on real AI demand in the market. In 2026, capex is expected to be roughly $200B. A depressed FCF is a near-term issue equaling high risk, high reward.

Metrics & Valuation

Let’s take a look at Amazon’s current return on invested capital (ROIC) because they have already been spending heavily on AI and are very capital intensive. Amazon’s EBIT is currently $80B, making their net operating profit after tax roughly $64B. We’ll divide this number by their total assets, cash, and operating liabilities, which are $818B, $86.8B, and $218B. Subtracting these numbers gives us $513 for the denominator. This makes Amazon’s ROIC roughly $64 / $513 = 12.5%. This is a solid percentage and is above their WACC, roughly 9% as an estimate, displaying solid growth creation. It is also important to note that this percentage is pulled down due to heavy spending, making Amazon’s future ROIC potentially higher if their investments pan out, which I have conviction they will because demand isn’t going anywhere.

In FY25, Amazon’s earnings per share were $7.17. Up from $5.53 the year prior and $2.90 in FY23.

As of this article, Amazon currently trades at roughly $200, making Amazon’s price-to-earnings ratio roughly 27x.

On a forward earnings basis, Amazon appears relatively expensive, trading at 25-26x earnings for 8% expected EPS growth, compared to peers like Meta Platforms (META), which offers significantly higher growth at a lower multiple, 17-18x and 28.74% expected growth. But there is nuance to this. Amazon’s earnings are temporarily depressed right now due to capex surges and their FCF dropping. This directly leads to EPS, margins, and ROIC dipping in the short term. Microsoft (MSFT) will also see a dip in P/E but with slower earnings growth of 5%, and Google (GOOGL) is expected to have a 24x multiple with 7% growth.

The difference between Amazon and a company like Meta who has a low forward P/E and high expected EPS growth, is that investments are happening alongside strong earnings growth for Meta, while investments are happening at the expense of current earnings for Amazon. Amazon’s relatively lower forward EPS growth compared to peers such as Meta is not a reflection of weaker demand but rather a result of its more aggressive reinvestment strategy.

This does not cause a value investor like myself to fret. Rather, I am even more optimistic. Amazon looks like it’s trading at more of a premium due to a high forward P/E, making Amazon less desirable in some investors eyes. But as some investors become cautious about this stock, I’ll remain bullish. FY25 and Q4 2025 financials were solid, but in the short term, earnings and FCF may be depressed, and that’s more than OK because Amazon is a long-term investment capitalizing on real AI demand in the market while still having a solid cloud and retail business.

Forward Looking & Consensus

Briefly looking at Amazon’s consensus EPS estimates, we can see that over the next year, EPS growth will be slower compared to expected future years due to heavy investments.

As FCF and capex spending normalize, EPS will increase as a result.

Taking a look at some analysts' expectations, most major investment firms are bullish on Amazon and expect an outperformance. Citigroup, UBS, Barclays, and BMO Capital expect Amazon’s stock price in a year’s time to be roughly from $285 to the highest estimate of $310. Currently, the average analyst price target is $281.34.

I agree with analysts' estimates for reasons I have explained throughout this article but will reiterate: strong financials, heavy investments in real AI demand, solid and growing AWS, retail, and advertising segments, and cloud market dominance.

Risk To Thesis

The primary risk to this thesis is that Amazon’s elevated capex may not generate the level of returns currently implied by management and investor expectations. The company is investing aggressively in AI infrastructure, with capex expected to reach approximately $200 billion in 2026, following already significant spending in 2025.

If demand for AI and cloud services does not scale as anticipated, or if pricing pressure grows due to competition from Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud, Amazon may face a long period of depressed FCF and weaker ROIC. In this scenario, the current reinvestment cycle would not translate into the expected future earnings expansion, and Amazon’s valuation could remain compressed or decline.

Additionally, while AWS remains the largest cloud provider, their growth advantage has narrowed relative to peers, increasing the risk that Amazon must continue spending heavily simply to maintain its market position. This could result in structurally lower margins than investors expect.

If Amazon fails to achieve sufficient scale, their earnings and FCF may not normalize as expected, making Amazon’s premium valuation hard to justify.

Conclusion

Amazon may look expensive on the surface, but that’s because it’s aggressively investing in AI and cloud infrastructure rather than maximizing short-term profits. In my view, that’s exactly what you want to see.

Current earnings and FCF don’t fully reflect the company’s true earning power. As these investments begin to scale, I expect meaningful reacceleration in growth.

With strong fundamentals, clear leadership in AWS, and multiple high-margin growth drivers, I see the current setup as a compelling long-term opportunity and a Strong Buy.

data.title: Celsius: Chug Or Sip?
data.description:
As an avid energy drink enjoyer, I tend to try out many new flavors and brands when they come out. One brand that I have tried many flavors of is Celsius (CELH), which is the subject of this article. Celsius is a challenger in the energy drink market, setting itself apart by using more natural ingredients. The question that will be explored in this article is whether this is enough to make it a good investment.

Company Overview

Celsius is a Nevada-incorporated, Florida-headquartered energy drink company. The company's main brand, Celsius, was founded in 2005 but did not really get into prominence until the Covid lockdowns. Where other challengers managed to get a foot in the door in the energy drink market but subsequently failed (such as Bang), Celsius managed to take a decent market share from Monster (MNST) and Red Bull. Last year, the company also completed the acquisitions of Alani Nu and Rockstar (from Pepsi (PEP)), making it the largest competitor to the incumbents.

Even though the acquired brands are also active in the energy drink sector, they are to an extent complementary. The Celsius brand mostly focuses on fitness/wellness consumers, whereas Alani Nu mainly focuses on female consumers and Rockstar on the more "traditional" energy drink consumers.

While the ready-to-drink (RTD) category is by far the largest, the company also sells on-the-go powder & hydration sticks. The advantage of the sticks is that the costs to manufacture are cheaper as they do not require the aluminum cans, nor do they need to add the sparkling water. Personally, I prefer the sticks, as they tend to be highly concentrated, and 1 stick can make up to 2 liters of energy drink. Besides the energy drinks and derivatives, the company also sells nutrition and wellness products. This is the result of the acquisition of Alani Nu, which was already selling things like protein drinks and pre-workout.

Most of these products aren't produced in-house, though, as the company primarily uses co-packers for manufacturing (it does have its own facility due to the acquisition of Big Beverages in 2024). In the US & Canada, the manufacturing and distribution are handled by Pepsi (for Alani Nu since Q4 2025 and for Celsius since 2024). Internationally, it tends to be handled by the Suntory Group, a Japanese company that also holds the license for Pepsi Japan.

Pepsi Partnership

While briefly touched upon above, Celsius and Pepsi have a strong partnership also called "the Captaincy." The captaincy was the result of a transaction between the two companies in which Celsius acquired the rights to be the strategist of the energy drink sales in North America, bought Rockstar, and issued Pepsi convertible preferred stock. Under the Captaincy, Pepsi is required to use "commercially reasonable efforts to sell and distribute the products of the Celsius brands." The proceeds were approximately $935.8 million, of which approximately $307 million for Rockstar. As a result of the newly issued convertible preferred stock, Pepsi is also allowed to add an additional board member to the board of Celsius.

Personally, I am a bit on the fence about this transaction. The reason for this is that, effectively, Celsius paid Pepsi $599 million to sell Celsius' own product. While it obviously is a bit more nuanced, it seems like Pepsi got the better end of the deal.

Governance

In terms of governance, the company had 10 directors at the end of 2025, two of whom are linked to Pepsi (Christy Jacoby and John Short). Out of the other 8, 7 are independent. A board with more independent directors tends to be favorable, as they are more objective (they are not judging their own work), are more likely to protect minority shareholders, and tend to focus more on the longer term.

Another interesting thing to look at is insider activity. Over the past 12 months, there were significantly more sales than buys. Additionally, many of the shares bought were the result of RSUs, options, etc. At the same time, the CEO has a 10b5-1 trading plan for 792k shares. This allows him to sell on a predetermined schedule, as he has access to material non-public information.

For a company that, to an extent, signals that it is undervalued, the executives do not seem to put their money where their mouth is, which is a bit disappointing.

Industry and Competitive Landscape

As mentioned before, Celsius is the third-largest company in the energy drink sector. The largest company in 2025 was Red Bull, with a market share of 35.9%, trailed by Monster with a market share of 27.3% (ex. other brands like Reign). Behind Celsius (including all brands at 19.8%, as mentioned before), there are a bunch of smaller companies which account for the remaining 17%.

The energy drink market is also growing, and according to research from Ipsos, approximately 19% of Americans consume energy drinks with caffeine, and 3% drink energy drinks without caffeine. One of the reasons why energy drinks and other caffeinated drinks have won in popularity is the relatively high price of coffee.

The reason why consumers drink caffeine is to get them through the morning or the afternoon. Many of the younger generation (18-34) wish caffeinated drinks came in smaller sizes. Something that is especially favorable for Red Bull, which mainly sells 8.4fl oz cans, and to a lesser extent Celsius & Alani Nu (12fl oz) versus Monster & Rockstar (16 fl oz).

Another trend that is favorable for Celsius is that approximately 58% of US consumers are paying attention to the ingredients in the drinks (Celsius claims to have natural ingredients), 52% are willing to pay more for drinks that support their fitness goals, and 66% tend to choose lower-sugar and lower-calorie drinks.

On a generation-by-generation basis, the highest usage of energy drinks can be found among Gen Z (18-28 years old), followed by Millennials (29-44). Interestingly, alcohol usage is much lower.

7 Powers

I recently read the book the 7 Powers by Hamilton Helmer. I think it does an excellent job in explaining what could set a company apart from its competitors. For Celsius, I have identified two main ones:

Counter positioning: This used to be the main one for Celsius. It positioned itself on the opposite side of Monster and Red Bull by having natural products, low sugar content, and a different target group (fitness and wellness). Red Bull and Monster couldn't easily change this without undermining their core brands. This was especially successful at the beginning when Celsius took a large chunk of market share. Red Bull and Monster have since adapted, and by acquiring Rockstar, Celsius is no longer only focused on the health-conscious consumer. While the brand is still doing well in terms of counter positioning, its power is weakening. At the same time, the company is getting scale, so it might be that the company is losing its counter-positioning power and instead will have a scale economies power.

Branding: Branding in the 7 Powers is not merely awareness; it is the ability to charge a premium based on how the product makes consumers feel. For Celsius, this is especially how consumers feel with regard to, for example, fitness. People that drink a Celsius energy drink might feel like they are making a better choice than when they would grab a Monster. This is not easily replicable, as the company has built this image over time. For Alani Nu, the branding has the same prevalence but towards a female audience. The only brand of the company that does not possess this power is Rockstar, which has been degrading over time. The strength of the brand also makes me less worried about the Kirkland knockoff from Costco (COST). In Europe, Lidl, Aldi, and many other grocery stores have knockoffs of Monster and Red Bull which are similar to a degree but not the same. Even though these knockoffs are significantly cheaper, Monster and Red Bull still hold the largest market shares. As such, the recent decline in share price seems like an overreaction.

Financials

After establishing a qualitative understanding of the business, it is time to dive into the numbers. I prefer to look at a few key components: revenue growth, margins, and debt.

Revenue growth

For Celsius, the growth rate has been quite volatile over the past few years. Initially it was growing very fast, coming from a low base. When it switched to Pepsi for its distribution and Pepsi made a mistake in terms of how much to order, the growth rate suffered. From that perspective, 2025 looks much better, but it was influenced by two acquisitions. Without the acquisitions, the revenue growth rate would be much lower. Going forward I expect the growth rate to be much lower as well due to the size the company has reached.

Margins

In terms of margins, I tend to compare it to the closest competitors. For energy drinks, a lot of emphasis is placed on the gross margins. Unfortunately, Celsius' margin definition is different than Monster's, and as such they are not fully comparable. Effectively, Celsius includes freight costs in their margins, and Monster does not. If we adjusted for this, then Celsius' margin would be 55.3% vs. Monster's 55.8%. Another thing that is a relatively good predictor of margins is the cost of Aluminum. In the graph below, I charted them against each other, and while the relationship isn't as steady as it used to be, there is still a correlation.

Debt

The final thing to look at is the debt of the company. To acquire Alani Nu, the company went from zero debt to approximately $700m in gross debt. The company has a BB- rating, meaning they are below investment grade, something we do not like to see. They have already refinanced the initial term loan at a rate 75 bp below the initial term loan, which is a positive. Unfortunately, the debt is still at a variable rate, and a 1 percentage point increase in rates adds approximately $6.9m in annual interest expenses. On the other hand, the maturity ladder is manageable, as they only pay around $7m a year in principal.

Valuation

While understanding the company is useful, you also need to know if what you are paying is reasonable. To do this, I like to use three methods: a DCF calculation, an APV calculation, and trading multiples.

DCF

For Celsius, I forecasted revenue growth based on their market share in the different markets they are active in. For the US, I also split it between the different brands. For 2026 I expect a slightly higher earnings growth due to the fact that both Rockstar and Alani Nu will be included for the entire year. I also expect a slightly higher growth in a few years due to more focus on internationalization. For now I have put a slightly higher growth rate in 2028, but this could be earlier or later. I also expect an improvement in cost; last year was impacted by integration costs, and this year I expect costs to be impacted by higher freight and aluminum costs. In the end this led to the following forecast:

The WACC was determined based on the risk-free rate, the company’s cost of debt, and a risk premium. I use an average approach based on the capital structure of the peers that were also used in the financial section, the company itself, the beta of the company, and the beta of peers, and this led to a WACC of 7.23%. The relatively lower WACC compared to its historical cost of equity is the result of the slightly larger peers that are included (such as Monster, Coca-Cola (KO), and Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP)) and expected stabilization of growth over time.

With an expected growth in perpetuity of 2.9%, slightly below GDP, the DCF produced the following share price estimate:

As DCF models are sensitive to input parameters, I also created a sensitivity table, which looks like this:

APV

The APV model is similar to the DCF but takes into account tax shields and distress probabilities. For Celsius, given their BB- rating, the distress probability is 19.7%. The potential enterprise value is quite high due to the large amount of goodwill on the balance sheet.

For the APV, I also created a sensitivity table to see what a change in input parameter would do to the estimated value.

Multiples

For the multiples, I used the same peers as for the WACC (MNST, KO, KDP, National Beverages (FIZZ), Molson Coors (TAP), and Vita Coco (COCO)). Forward multiples are based on analyst expectations. Taking the median multiple based on the company’s peers leads to a share price of $31.61. One can argue that this undervalues the company, as many of these do not show the same growth as Celsius. Which I agree with. As such, I assigned the multiples a lower weight in the final valuation.

If we take the average of the DCF method, the APV method, and the combined multiples median (at a 0.5 weight), we arrive at a price target of $43.01, or approximately 26.7% above the price at the moment of writing.

Catalysts

A company without a catalyst can be trading at a cheap valuation for a long time. As such, it is important to look for events that can increase the stock price in the short to mid-term.

Alani Nu growth

One of the main growth stories for Celsius has been the acquisition of Alani Nu. I think that the growth story for Alani Nu is far from done as more (female) consumers are switching from other caffeine drinks (such as coffee) to energy drinks. As the brand is very active with female influencers, these new customers might be more drawn to the Alani Nu brand. Personally, I have to admit that I also prefer the flavors that they bring out under the Alani Nu brand over the flavors they bring out under the Celsius brand.

International

Another catalyst, which is more for the mid-term, is the under-penetration of the international markets. Celsius holds very small market shares outside of North America, and these markets tend to grow faster as energy drinks are not as widespread as in the US. I think that once the company starts to increase its focus abroad and start selling Alani Nu, it will be able to gain some market share in faster-growing markets.

Risks

Celsius brand is stalling, and Rockstar does not help

One of the main risks right now is that the Celsius brand seems to stall. Full-year 2025 core Celsius brand revenue growth was only 7.5%. Additionally, this 7.5% was also compared to the year in which Pepsi made some ordering mistakes. As such, the comps were already very low, and they barely beat it. The acquired Rockstar brand does not help much, as the brand has been slowly degrading over time and needs a revamp. It remains to be seen if Celsius is able to turn this around.

Variable-rate debt

Another risk given the current macro environment is the variable rate debt. As mentioned before, a 1 percentage point increase in rates adds an additional $6.9m in interest expenses. As I do not expect the macro environment to get much better any time soon, there is a high chance that the interest expense will increase significantly in the near future.

Conclusion

Celsius is a fast-growing beverage company that has recently improved its position as the third-largest energy drink company by acquiring two competitors. The company has also deepened its collaboration with Pepsi, for which it now controls the distribution and promotion strategy of the energy drink segment. Nevertheless, this came at a cost, as it had to pay for these rights, and it also acquired the degrading Rockstar brand.

The partnership with Pepsi has also led to more volatile revenue growth, despite a growing market and market share, and margins remain heavily impacted by aluminum prices. Furthermore, the acquisitions have led to a higher debt load, for which the company pays a variable rate.

Despite all of this, the company is operating from a position of strength with strong brand power and a strong but decreasing counter-positioning power. Even so, the stock seems to be undervalued, with a conservative estimate of $43.01 per share. Catalysts include further growth in the Alani Nu brand and international expansion, while risks are the declining growth in the core Celsius brand and the acquisition of Rockstar and the exposure to variable rate debt. Taking all of this into account, I think that the positives outweigh the negatives and assign the company a buy.

data.title: Oil Price Surge Hits German Consumers As Headline Inflation Reaches A Two-Year High
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro

Soaring energy prices have pushed German headline inflation close to 3%

This shouldn’t be a surprise to anyone. As the war in the Middle East has entered its fifth week and both energy prices and uncertainty remain high, German headline inflation reached its highest level since early 2024 in March.

While the national inflation measure came in at 2.7% year-on-year, from a still innocent 1.9% YoY in February, the European measure surged to 2.8% YoY, from 2.0% YoY. At 1.2% month-on-month, March saw the largest monthly price increase since 2022.

At the same time, however, today’s numbers also showed that for the time being, the inflation shock remains limited to energy prices, as core inflation and services inflation remained unchanged at 2.5% YoY and 3.2% YoY, respectively.

First inflation wave clearly on its way

Oil prices have always been the most important and direct link between geopolitical developments and the real economy. While the war in the Middle East and the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz provide further evidence of shifting geopolitics and will have longer-term implications for the European economy, the rise in energy prices is already very real. In Germany, if gasoline prices remained at their current levels until the end of the year, the loss in purchasing power for consumers would already be larger than in 2022.

Today’s inflation data shows that a first inflation wave is clearly on its way. While currently available regional data suggests that the inflation surge in March was mainly driven by energy prices, knock-on effects on transportation costs, food prices and other industrial products will follow. The only question is whether this will be a single, time-limited wave or whether it will eventually also lead to a de-anchoring of inflation expectations and higher wages. However, even if it is only ‘one’ large inflation wave, German inflation should increase further, remaining in the 3% to 4% range for most of the year.

What's next for the ECB?

When looking at possible next steps for the European Central Bank, markets have started to price in up to four rate hikes this year. Indeed, the central bank made a hawkish pivot at the last policy meeting – but we think that markets are too much guided by an overly simplistic reading of the 2022 episode and the narrative that the ECB was far too late reacting to an oil price shock. In our view, however, there are important differences between the current situation and 2022.

Back then, the ECB was emerging from an extremely accommodative stance and normalising policy from negative interest rates and quantitative easing. With hindsight, the biggest policy mistake was probably the delayed response to an energy price shock that ultimately morphed into a broader inflation surge. Learning from that episode, though, does not mean a rate hike is imminent.

As long as the energy price shock remains broadly contained – including first‑round knock-on effects – it remains far from certain that the ECB will react at all. For rate hikes to come back to the table, the Bank would need to see a rise in inflation expectations and a broadening of inflationary pressures across the economy. So far, the war in the Middle East has instead weighed on business and consumer confidence. Meanwhile, the labour market is entering this energy shock in a weaker position than in 2022, and governments’ fiscal pockets are more constrained, making large‑scale stimulus to offset higher energy prices less likely.

Learning from the Easter Bunny

Maybe market participants can learn some lessons from the Easter Bunny. Due to surging prices for cocoa last year, chocolate Easter bunnies have seen their prices increase by up to 30% compared with 2025. A textbook pass-through of higher production costs, one could think. However, this analyst noticed that German supermarkets, at least, have already started special sales of chocolate Easter bunnies ahead of the holiday, rather than the traditional post-Easter dump sale – suggesting that overly high prices can eventually break consumer demand. Maybe that's a first indication that household balance sheets are simply so stretched that any energy price pass-through will be much less pronounced this year than in 2022.

Three potential pain points for the ECB

Looking ahead, three potential pain points remain for the ECB: a psychological one, i.e., headline inflation above 4%, reviving uncomfortable memories of 2022; an analytical one, with core inflation above 3%, signalling broader price pressures; and a credibility one, i.e., a surge in survey‑based inflation expectations, which would constrain the ECB's room for inaction.

Even if the latest developments in the war and in energy prices have started to shift our base case scenario more towards our adverse scenario, we find it hard to see the ECB moving at the next meeting at the end of April. By then, there will be no new forecasts, and only limited data available: March inflation, a handful of country inflation releases for April, and initial estimates of first‑quarter GDP on the day of the meeting. In all honesty, that does not look sufficient to move the needle, unless the ghosts of 2022 are really keeping policymakers awake at night.

For the June meeting, the story is different. Simply put, if we are still talking about the war in the Middle East and high energy prices by then, a rate hike is clearly possible. However, this is not our base case, as we expect the Strait of Hormuz to reopen before then.

Transitory for now but broadening of price shock could still trigger rate hike

All in all, today's German inflation data shows that, for the time being, the new inflation wave stemming from the war in the Middle East is 'only' an energy price shock. Any ECB reaction to this new price shock will clearly depend on whether soaring energy prices will find their way into the rest of the economy or not. Needless to say that a broadening of inflationary pressures and a de-anchoring of inflation expectations could trigger an ECB rate hike. For now, however – at the risk of using a forbidden word, at least at the ECB – the energy price shock falls under the label of 'transitory'.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: S&P 500: 10 Biggest Stocks Look So Bad, I Can't Be Bullish
data.description:
I am a risk manager first. I frankly do not care how much I can make if it means I have a significant chance of losing big along the way. If it is a choice of “go big or go home,” I’ll see you at home. However, I firmly believe that attitude does NOT prevent the pursuit of long-term returns. That’s been my experience operating this way for decades.

Prioritizing risk management is also not about making constant bets about whether I should be in the market or out. I’m always in and out, so to speak. I call it “playing offense and defense at the same time. And depending on which of my accounts I’m talking about, that can take the form of:

  • A 2-ETF portfolio (SPY and BIL only), rotating among them so that the total allocation equals 100%. Frankly, that type of simplicity, as I’ve written about before and will again soon, is proving to be much more competitive with all the most aggressive strategies than anyone (including me) would have thought.
  • A 3-ETF portfolio (SPY, VIXY, BIL) in which I go beyond the long stocks plus cash setup above and allow myself a small allocation to VIXY, or a similar volatility ETF. Markets can shift gears as fast as a social post now. So to have a “disaster” insurance vehicle in there at all times, even if it is just 1-2% of the portfolio much of the time, can be a game-changer.
  • A 10-ETF portfolio, but where the basket of 10 ETFs is “permanent.” That means I aim not to change them very much. But I own all 10 all the time, just with preset upper, lower, and “neutral” limits. I wrote about this recently, and it was about my most popular article ever on this site. So I get the idea that the concept is well-received.
  • Option collars: I wrote a lot about these during early 2025, and I’m warming up to them again, since high volatility helps the cause here. SPY is something I collar regularly, though more and more, I see “alpha” potential by, for example, owning 100 shares, selling 1 call option (or maybe none), and buying 3-5 far out-of-the-money puts. This is the “convexity” effect, whereby the protection is cheap, and if a small drop in SPY occurs, the options will probably expire worthless unless I do something with them soon enough. But if the market really cracks, the gains might be much greater than whatever I lose on the underlying SPY position.

There are many more ways to “take risk out of taking risk.” And based on audience feedback, I will angle my near-term coverage toward the ones you collectively want to learn more about. I’m here to transition my knowledge. Hopefully, it helps you, and it helps me to put these thoughts down. I’ve always found that writing it down makes it easier to execute.

So that’s a quick review of some of the many ways I’m both managing risk and not giving up on making a ton of profit over time. And while I have a well-documented history of making money in down markets (2026 to date included), the “partner” of whatever portfolio decisions any of us make is the obvious one: market conditions.

Even an average S&P 500 environment allows those who play offense and defense at the same time to generate positive returns. And in a friendly environment, as we had during most of 2023-2025, everyone makes money. Or at least has a fighting chance to.

The S&P 500 Index right now: super risky, even by my conservative standards

But the S&P 500 is likely past that reliable “buy the dips” era. I never count out any possible outcome. However, this article aims to show the evidence I’ve collected that this is beyond a simple case of “elevated risk.”

Why? Because when I look at the top stocks by weight, the ones that drive the S&P 500, those in the top 10 (more than 35% of the market), I just cannot make the case that this will do anything other than get worse before it gets better. I think the odds are very strongly in that direction. In fact, while I’ll just mention it briefly here, I do have a 5,000-year-end target for the S&P 500. With each passing week, the technical picture leans a bit more strongly toward that being a viable target.

This is more about market structure than anything else. The charts I follow every day, and have since the 1980s, are about as challenging a picture for bulls as I’ve ever seen. Because the rest of the market is just not capable of making up for the weak-looking pictures I see, which are the charts of the 10 biggest stocks by weight.

Here’s the daily chart of the S&P 500. It looks just awful. I’ll likely drill down to that 20-day moving average in a future article, because it doesn’t look like this often. That long tail down formation is the type of thing I only see at the inception of major market downturns. So that’s one box checked for “this is likely to get worse.” Can it bounce? Of course! But selling rallies is different from buying dips. And the former is clearly replacing the latter lately.

Here’s a weekly price chart of the S&P 500 going back 5 years. Not far enough back to include the 2020 COVID-19 crash and recovery. But there’s plenty here to go on.

I numbered the last 3 times the current setup could be seen. Specifically, the 20-week moving average has rolled over and is now trending down. And the 50-week moving average is about to join it. In the bottom section of the chart, I show the Percentage Price Oscillator (PPO). Most non-technicians have probably heard of the “MACD” momentum indicator. PPO is similar, but more accurate, in my opinion.

I’ve labeled those past periods that looked like the current setup.

1 = 2022, when stocks and bonds fell in sync for the first time in 40 years. It only took 4 years to happen again. It’s happening right now. This is why “60/40” and other traditional allocation strategies are failing. And likely will continue to for a while. Even if the war in Iran settles down soon, the inflation and fiscal neglect that are ingrained in US policy are not reversing so quickly.

2 = This turned out to be “only” a 10% drop in SPX, and it was quick. The PPO stopped falling right about where it is now. I take that as an indication that this is a pretty critical couple of weeks coming up right here. It’s either hang on here or take another leg down.

3 = last year’s tariff mess, which was saved by a social post. That could happen again any time, and in fact, I’m accounting for that in my investing. Whether it is an out-of-the-money call option or the option collar setup discussed above, if we get that type of instant resolution to a downward market, I want to be prepared to have my bearish bias offset immediately. If I’ve learned anything from decades of doing this, it is that you should always be ready for the instant and lasting change in trend. That bottom happens in a few days. Yes, offense AND defense, at the same time.

4 = here we are again. Similar look, but this time around, the likelihood of a Fed save or an instant rebound in inflation, employment, or housing is more remote.

12 ways to lose money in the stock market, all at once

There is no question in my view that this is THE biggest misunderstanding prevailing in the minds of self-directed investors. The belief that diversification is still like it used to be. It simply is not.

This recent chart shows the latest incidence of this. Pick your poison, as they say. 12 different ways to own equities, ranging from S&P 500 cap-weighted and equal-weighted to small cap, international, concentrated, growth, value, and covered call writing. I even threw in a zero-days-to-expiration S&P 500 ETF. And the best performer of the 12, SPYD, which is the 80 highest-yielding stocks in the S&P 500.

Sure, there might be some temporary feeling of relief when the market falls 8%, and you are down 5-6%. But the point is, the correlation among markets is now sky-high. And that’s a matter of how the market is structured. The top 10 stocks and the next 10-15 beyond that are what control the behavior of the rest. Not every stock, industry, and sector at every moment.

It is the prevailing trend I use to set my broader approach. And right now, there’s no place to hide except in inverse ETFs and cash. That means it’s a great time to understand how to use them.

As for that top 10, the list is familiar to any equity investor. This shows both Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(GOOGL) tickers, so I’ll note that JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) is 11th by market cap and makes the top 10 in my book since I combine the pair of Alphabet tickers.

The Seeking Alpha Quant Factor Grades, presented here in 2 sets to cover the top 10 companies list, are signaling anything but “buy the dip.” Valuations are high, growth is mixed, and there is a growing possibility that all the AI spending, while it might deliver over time, has left expectations impossibly high for the market to reward.

Conclusion: Look out below, and manage risk

So yes, the top 10 are a problem. Because they carry too much weight in the index to not drive the market either way. And while I’ve not shown the charts of the individual stocks here, they are universally awful. Even the ones that have been steady as opposed to already broken, names like NVDA, are on the verge of joining the vast majority and finding lower levels.

So, I’ve given you the evidence. Some technical, a bit of fundamental, and a lot of it structural. Do not mistake this for my not wanting to find stocks and ETFs to buy. But for many months now, since I started flagging many of the aforementioned issues last October, the mission has been the same:

1. Don’t force anything. Cash yields are not bad.

2. Own equity exposure, but in ways that are hedged at all times.

3. Realize that the long run-up in a concentrated group of mega-cap stocks has not only made the S&P 500 structurally unsound to a historic degree. It has also created a “giant sucking sound” that has made it so stock-picking is much less fruitful, unless you are trading, not investing. Correlation is too high.

This could change. But for now, I’m playing the market I have, not the one many wish we had. That’s not being a bull or a bear, but a realist.

data.title: Jack Henry & Associates: Quality Remains, But Growth Is Slowing
data.description:
Introduction

In my view, Jack Henry & Associates, Inc. (JKHY) today is not a growth story anymore; it's becoming a stable infrastructure company whose current valuation already reflects quality, though it doesn't leave much room for further growth.

Jack Henry & Associates is currently demonstrating solid operational momentum, which is backed by the 2026 Q2 results. The company’s revenues reached $619.3 million, which is 7.9% growth compared to last year, and the earnings per share jumped 28.6% to $1.72. The most important achievement is the operating margin expansion to 25.7%, compared to 21.4% last year, achieved due to strict cost savings control and higher deconversion tax revenues.

Even though the company increased the full-year 2026 EPS forecast to the $6.61-6.72 range, investors need to value JKHY through a consistent growth prism: the expected revenue growth is reaching around 6%. The current P/E ratio is around 24, which is lower than the recorded P/E of around 31 at the end of 2024, though it still remains higher than the diversified financial services sector average of 17.6.

The cloud services segment, making up 33% of the revenues, grew 8%, which shows a successful client migration, though the bank sector's consolidation in the US remains a structural risk, which could limit the number of new contracts in the long term. The payment segment, which is generating 37.5% of revenues, also had a growth of 8%, where volume growth is rapid in the instant payments area (Zelle, RTP, FedNow). The debt level was significantly reduced to $20 million, compared to $150 million last year, which strengthened the balance sheet and decreased interest costs. P/B ratio is at 5.00, which suggests a high market premium for the company's intellectual property and stable cash flows. A 1.55% dividend yield and 35% payout ratio show a sustainable but conservative capital return policy, which is oriented to technological renewal rather than aggressive growth.

Business Overview

Jack Henry & Associates operates as a critical technological infrastructure provider for the U.S. community banks and credit unions, serving more than 7500 financial institutions. In my view, this shows a strong market position, though also a huge dependency on the regional banks segment, which in the long term is facing structural pressure. The business model is strictly segmented into core banking systems, payment processing, and complementary solutions. The company maintains a very stable profile, where over 90% of the revenues are recurring, which ensures the predictable cash flow even in macroeconomic uncertainty. Though if we look from a different point of view, the recurring revenues also limit growth ability, because expansion depends more on already existing clients rather than new ones.

Fiscal year 2026 revenue guidelines are reaching $2.51-2.53 billion, recording a 7.6% growth, which is supported by the complementary segment’s growth of 9.6% and the payments segment’s growth of 8%. Currently, there is an ongoing strategic product rotation from the on-premise systems to the new services, such as SaaS and Public Cloud architecture. In my opinion, this is a mandatory strategic step, though for now it hasn't shown that it would significantly accelerate the company's overall growth. Banno Digital Platform became the main tool, allowing 78% of core customers to migrate to debit, this way increasing operating efficiency and client loyalty, which is reaching 98%.

Even though in 2026 Q2 the operating margin increased to 25%, exceeding the 24% yearly forecast, the P/E ratio at around 24 is 28% lower than the ten-year median of 33.1. Such valuation contraction suggests that the market doesn't want to pay a premium price for stable but slow, single-digit growth, especially when competition from the fintech side of things requires greater investment in research. Financial stability is confirmed by the 2026 signing of a new $1 billion credit line and the increased dividend by 6%, though share valuation remains sensitive to any product migration pace slowing.

Valuation

Jack Henry & Associates valuation remains unique because of the company's niche position, though compared to the wide financial technology sector, the share price is not low. The current forward P/E ratio is reaching 24.0, which is a high premium compared to American Express Company (AXP), which is at 17x forward P/E, and even a higher premium compared to PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL), whose forward P/E is at 8.5x. In my view, JKHY's multiplier reflects more of a business stability rather than growth potential, and in the current market, investors don't pay a premium for just stability. Though this difference in valuations is justified by JKHY's business model's resilience: the company services community banks, whose technological migration is slower, but contracts are way longer, ensuring 90% recurring revenues.

EV/EBITDA is reaching 15.9, which is also significantly higher than the sector median, showing that the market still values JKHY as a safe asset with lower volatility risk. However, looking at 2026 fiscal forecasts, the PEG ratio around 2-3 signals a possible overvaluation, because EPS growth is forecasted at 9.6% and fundamentally doesn't justify such a high multiplier. Compared to the five-year historical P/E average of 33.8, the share price seems undervalued, though this decline is systematic, reflecting investors need for higher returns when interest rates are higher. Dividend yield is reaching 1.2%, which is similar to peers; that's why JKHY's valuation premiums in the market are treated as the price of security for low leverage.

Risks

In my view, the main Jack Henry & Associates risk in 2026 arises from the accelerating consolidation in the US banking sector, which directly lowers the company's client base. The latest company data shows that forecasted deconversion revenues from contracts have increased to $28 million for the whole of 2026. This indicator reflects situations when JKHY clients are acquired by larger financial institutions, which are usually using competitive systems.

Even though the latest quarter's earnings showed a revenue growth of 7.9%, in the long term, the yearly revenue growth forecast is slower, at around 4.6-5.8%, which is way lower than the overall technology sector's average. The competitive environment is getting stronger because of companies such as FIS and Fiserv applying bundling strategies, as well as new, aggressive cloud-native providers, for example, Thought Machine or Mambu, entering the market. Also, the company's clients, which are regional banks and credit unions, are losing market share; fintech companies are capturing a significantly higher share of new account growth compared to traditional banks. This creates a risk to JKHY's business model, because the declining number of end users in traditional institutions in the long term will lower the revenues of processing transactions, which in 2026 Q2 made up 44.2% of the whole company's revenues.

From my perspective, this risk is not a short-term one but a structural one, which in the long term can essentially change JKHY's growth trajectory. Specifically, the weakening of the customer base, not the technological lag, seems to be the main long-term source of pressure. That is why even the stable short-term results can be misleading, because the core business dynamic is slowly worsening.

Additional pressure for margins is created by the necessity to increase investment in cybersecurity and artificial intelligence integration, seeking to maintain technological competitiveness, which could limit operational margin development above the achieved 25.7% threshold. Even though the P/E currently has dropped to around the 24x levels, this indicates more of the market's reorientation to a slow growth scenario rather than an opportunity to buy, because the company no longer justifies its previously held growth stock status.

Thesis Summary

In my view, Jack Henry & Associates is currently a quality stock, but not a growth stock anymore. The company has a very strong foundation, a high recurring revenue level of 90%, stable cash flows, and a clear role as critical infrastructure for community banks. This allows it to maintain high margins and a relatively safe business model even in a more complex macro environment.

However, the main problem is the growth. Single-digit revenue growth and 9-10% EPS growth are not enough to justify the current 24 P/E multiplier in my view, especially when the market today requires more growth at this price. Also, structural factors, which are US banks consolidation and fintech competitiveness, raise the real risk in the long-term client base expansion.

Overall, I view this stock as a defensive quality play, but with a limited upside. The current discount to historical multipliers reflects the market’s shift to a low-growth narrative rather than a clear buying opportunity. For this reason, my position is neutral; this is not a bad company, but currently it is better for capital preservation than for aggressive portfolio growth.

data.title: Intel Still Leans On A Fragile CPU Business
data.description:
Intel Corporation (INTC) is back on my radar after it was reported last week that the current CPU supply constraints due to the AI data center buildout led Intel and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) to hike CPU prices, impacting PC and server makers.

This pressure comes on top of the earlier memory crunch, which led many PC makers to increase prices across their offerings.

The main issue that I see is that Intel still relies heavily on its traditional CPU businesses to support earnings. Its DCAI business is still far from the CCG segment in terms of both revenue and operating income.

My disappointment with the company is mainly due to the fact that it is unable to satisfy the high demand for server CPUs. On top of that, many analysts expect the PC market to contract this year by as much as mid-single digits, which will directly impact the CCG segment.

On the positive side, I now see two catalysts in the foundry business that could revive the optimism around this stock, despite the $2.5B operating loss in Q4 2025. In my view, any signed external customer (more likely in H2 than H1 this year) for 18A or 14A could meaningfully change sentiment and resume the bull run that began in August last year.

In the meantime, I see raw fundamentals and a macro that doesn't support the consumer side of the CCG business. I discuss more below.

Core x86 CPU Business Is Fragile

Why does this even matter in the first place, anyway?

Well, the company still depends heavily on x86 CPUs from an earnings perspective.

Last quarter, the Client Computing Group (CCG) recorded $2.2B in operating income. In comparison, the Data Center AI segment brought only $1.3B in operating income, and Intel Foundry lost $2.5B.

Let's leave the loss of Intel Foundry on the side for now and focus on the CPU business.

There are essentially two end markets here: PCs and servers.

Let's start with PCs. This is a highly cyclical market, and I have strong reasons to believe that it's near a cycle peak.

Why?

On top of the recent memory and storage inflation, it appears that CPUs are next in line, after some sources suggested that both Intel and AMD have recently told clients they will increase prices for all series of CPUs from March and April, respectively. In the last earnings call, CFO David Zinsner warned of the negative effect that the pricing of certain components could have on demand:

Rising component pricing is a dynamic we continue to watch closely, especially relative to the client market and could limit our revenue opportunity this year.

This risk is already materializing. In February, Lenovo told Reuters that it had raised prices to offset the increase in memory costs. As a side note, some analysts estimate that memory costs represent 25% to 30% of a PC's bill of materials. That's a lot, especially when looking at the PC DRAM price hike of over 100% quarter over quarter projected for Q1 2026.

Given these headwinds in the PC market, some analysts are now projecting the PC market to shrink at least 4.9% in 2026 after 8.1% growth in 2025.

That wouldn't be Intel's only problem in the PC segment. The other issue is related to its market share. As seen below, Intel has been persistently losing market share to AMD.

On the CPU server side, Intel has been caught off guard by the surging demand for server CPUs to the point that they were unable to fully meet that demand, even with factories running at full capacity.

I considered including below the remarks from Lip-Bu Tan during the last earnings call, which show the disappointment of the CEO:

But I'm also mindful of the challenges ahead of us and transparent about the areas that we are doing well and areas we need to improve. In the short term, I'm disappointed that we are not able to fully meet the demand in our markets.

Back in February, some reports suggested that Intel had warned Chinese customers of up to 6-month lead times for some server CPUs, with prices for Intel server products up 10%.

Aside from Intel's supply issues, last week, Arm Holdings (ARM) announced its own AI CPU for data centers, with Meta as lead partner and customers including OpenAI (OPENAI), Cloudflare, SAP, and SK Telecom. From a deployment perspective, Arm said that the chip would go into volume production in the second half of 2026, using TSMC's (TSM) 3-nm process node.

Even though I don't see the ARM headline as an immediate Intel share loss, the company now has one new player in the CPU space to worry about.

Good News For Foundry

Back in January, Intel said that two customers were evaluating the technical details of the 14A, with management expecting an answer in H2 2026 on whether external customers want to use it.

That's a nice catalyst ahead.

On top of that, the CFO recently said during a conference that the CEO, Lip-Bu Tan, now sees the 18A as a good node to offer to external customers.

And while Lip-Bu was, I think, thinking that we probably should focus on 14A as a foundry node and make 18A really just an internal node, now that we've got -- seen some real progress there, I think he's now starting to recognize that this is actually a good node to offer to external customers as well. And we've been getting some kind of inbound interest in 18A-P as a foundry node. So that's -- I think that's pretty positive.

That remark really raised my eyebrows, given that in the last earnings call, the CEO said that yields were still below his expectations:

My team and I are working tirelessly to drive efficiency and more output from our fabs. While yields are in line with our internal plans, they are still below what I want them to be.

Did they really improve that much to offer it to external customers? While I don’t know the answer to that question, it is definitely one of the key things I will be looking for in the next earnings season.

If the company secured even one unnamed external customer for the 18A (or 18A-P), I think shares could see the next leg of the current bull run.

In the meantime, Foundry is still a drag on the company, with a loss of about $2.5 billion at the operating level in Q4 2025 alone.

Valuation

If we look at 1-year non-GAAP earnings, the stock is trading at an 89x multiple. In comparison, AMD is trading at just 30x and ARM at 81x.

The only valuation metric where Intel excels is in its forward P/S. The company is trading at just 4x next year's sales, despite the 83% increase in its share price in the last year.

In comparison, AMD is trading at 7x forward sales and ARM at a 31x ratio.

Overall, I don't see Intel as a value play. In fact, after the investments last year from the U.S. government and SoftBank, the stock is largely trading based on its narrative and not its valuation.

Looking Ahead

From a technical perspective, I am quite disappointed with this stock. As seen below, the price is now breaking a long-term trend since August last year.

On the positive side, I still see some potential if the company is able to sign external foundry customers for either the 14A or the 18A process nodes. The fact that the CEO is now considering offering the 18A to external customers may be a sign that yields have improved significantly since the last earnings call.

That said, this is the only strong catalyst that I see for this stock. In the meantime, I see a deteriorating PC market, affected by both memory and CPU price hikes. This is likely to lead to a deterioration in consumer demand, especially if the lower-end consumer (globally, not just the U.S.) is now pressured by the hike in gas prices at the fuel pump after the conflicts in the Middle East.

Overall, regardless of any external customer announcements for the 14A or 18A nodes, I expect fundamentals to remain under pressure this year. Therefore, I reiterate my neutral rating on Intel.

data.title: Visa: Double-Digit EPS Growth Remains On Track, Shares Tag Key Technical Support
data.description:
There are several bearish narratives weaving through markets right now. Front and center is the war in Iran, but under the surface are the drastic perceived impacts from AI. Indeed, software stocks and fintech names have come under tremendous selling pressure. A brief post-Citrini Research reprieve was wiped away last week, and some of the largest S&P 500 stocks appear to be in the crosshairs.

Visa (V) is among them. I had a "Buy" rating on the now $563 billion market cap Financials sector stock back in July of 2025. Shares are down 15% since then, lagging the S&P 500 by some 16 percentage points.

Today, I reiterate a "Buy" rating. I am keeping my valuation the same—while near-term earnings are higher, the multiple should be compressed a bit as AI’s threat, while not a death knell, is legitimate. I’ll provide a refreshed technical view—V is right near long-term support, offering a buying opportunity.

Credit Card Stocks Lagging Amid Macro Uncertainty and Industry-Specific Jitters

Back in January, Visa reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q1 non-GAAP EPS of $3.17 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $3.14, while revenue of $10.9 billion, up 15% from the same period last year, was a material $210 million beat. Driving the beat was strong consumer spending over the holiday shopping season, despite a disappointing number of processed transactions.

Shares fell 3% in the session that followed, marking a third consecutive post-earnings decline and the worst performance back to July of 2024. Looking ahead to the April 28 release, the options market prices in a somewhat high 4.0% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the report. Implied volatility has ticked up to 31%, while short interest on the large-cap is very low, near 1%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, Visa delivered robust numbers to finish off 2025. The quarter’s strength was driven by growth in key business areas, including an 8% increase in payments volume, a 12% rise in total cross-border volume, and a 9% uptick in processed transactions. Additionally, Visa showed its commitment to shareholder returns by distributing $5.1 billion through share repurchases and dividends during the quarter.

Visa: Color On Quarter

Guidance was key, too. The company maintained a positive outlook for the balance of FY 2026, seeing low-double-digit growth in net revenue, operating expenses, and EPS on a non-GAAP adjusted currency basis.

For fiscal Q2 2026 specifically, Visa expects top-line growth to remain in the low double digits (thanks to increased marketing spend around the Olympics and World Cup), while operating expenses may rise at a mid-teens rate. Despite various global economic and regulatory risks, its early 2026 metrics—such as U.S. payments volume and processed transaction growth through late January—suggest continued stability and momentum in its core operations.

On the call, the management team suggested that earnings might land at the higher end of its guidance range due to a more favorable tax rate. Of course, that’s not necessarily indicative of improved operations. Regarding AI, Visa confirmed 17.5 billion tokens in circulation—it's not standing by as digital payments take off. Then, just last week, Visa unveiled a new value‑added service for its Digital Issuer Solutions business.

Visa: Q2 & 2026 Outlooks

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth remains on track. A low-teens bottom-line growth rate augurs for a continued premium valuation multiple, while revenue gains are likewise impressive. Of course, fears of a consumption slowdown could result in reduced earnings estimates.

Still, there have been a high 27 sell-side profit boosts in the past 90 days, compared to only six downgrades. Finally, Visa remains a profitability stalwart, as evidenced by $11.69 of free cash flow per share over the past 12 months.

Visa: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I am sticking with my previous fair value target. If we assume $13.50 of forward 12-month non-GAAP EPS and apply 27-28x P/E, shares should trade close to $370.

I increased my EPS figure based on the period shift while bringing down my multiple by more than two figures due to increased AI fears and the potential for reduced consumer spending. Keep in mind that the PEG ratio is almost 10% under the long-term average.

Visa: Remains a Premium Valuation, but Attractive PEG

Key risks for Visa include reduced consumer spending and lower cross-border travel trends, as the war in the Middle East raises energy costs. AI is another concern—the experienced management team must stay ahead of digital payment trends; otherwise, the P/E multiple gets compressed in a material way (just take a look at software stocks and cybersecurity industry shares).

Specifically on the Q1 call, analysts questioned the sustainability of Visa’s Commercial and Money Movement Solutions' performance, even with healthy Q1 results. To me, it was indicative of emerging doubts. Regulatory concerns are also in play, with the Trump administration clearly having its eyes on consumer affordability.

Elsewhere, Visa must keep its operating costs in check as it focuses on investments around the aforementioned sporting events.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q2 2026 earnings date of Tuesday, April 28, for AMC. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares attractive on valuation in my view, Visa’s technical situation offers more hope than you might think. Notice in the chart below that shares are now at key long-term support in the $290 to $299 zone. That was the peak from early 2024, and that's where V encountered buyers in April 2025.

To be clear, the long-term 200-day moving average is on the decline, suggesting that the bears now control the primary trend. What’s more, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph is ranging in a bearish zone between 30 and 50. I’d like to see price rise with an RSI uptrend.

Also take a look at the volume by price profile on the left side of the chart. Even if we get a relief rally, there’s now a high amount of shares traded up to near my fundamental price target. For now, the first resistance is possible near the falling 50-DMA — that has confluence with the November 2026 low. Above that, a downtrend line comes into the picture near $350.

V: Major Long-Term Support Just Below $300

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on Visa. The stock appears attractive on valuation, with very solid top and bottom line estimates persisting. The chart, meanwhile, reveals key long-term support in play ahead of the Q2 report in April.

data.title: John Hancock Freedom 529 College Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • U.S. stocks extended a historically fast rebound that began in early April, although upward momentum slowed in the final quarter of the year.
  • The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on AI, and the Fed's interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December.
  • Healthcare, a laggard for much of 2025, was the top-performing sector in the S&P 500 Index during Q4.

Market review and outlook

U.S. stocks gained 2.66% in the fourth quarter, according to the S&P 500 Index, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence, and the U.S. Federal Reserve's (Fed's) interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December. Amid this favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, the index closed the year just shy of its all-time high. Value stocks modestly outpaced growth in Q4, while large caps had only a slight advantage over small caps, as investors' appetite for risk waned versus the prior three months.

In the fourth quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensive-oriented healthcare sector (+12%), which particularly shined in November. The growth-oriented communication services sector rose about 7%, benefiting from the transformative potential of generative AI. All other groups lagged the index. Notably, IT, which represented 35% of the S&P 500 Index in Q4, rose about 1%.

International developed-market stocks advanced 4.89%, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index. The discrepancy between value and growth stocks was more pronounced in this index, again in favor of value over growth. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index notched a 4.72% rise. South Korea (+27%) and Taiwan (+10%) were two strong components with sizable representation in this index, bolstered by the strength in technology stocks in those markets. On the other hand, China finished the quarter with a roughly -7% return, giving back some of its gains since the April bottom.

U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds gained 1.1% in the fourth quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Quarterly performance was fueled by a combination of elevated starting yields and price appreciation, as bond prices rose amid falling interest rates and investor demand, supported by stable fundamentals.

The U.S. equity bull market is entering its fourth year, having put up double-digit gains in each of the past three, leaving investors wondering whether it can keep the streak alive. While multiyear stretches of positive equity returns are common, double-digit returns four years in row are few and far between. The naysayers point to elevated valuations, narrowness of the market, uncertainty around Fed policy amid upcoming leadership change, a softening labor market, and U.S. mid-term election risk. Yet, history has shown that bull markets don't die of old age alone, and while valuations are elevated, they're below prior cycle extremes. Bulls can also point to still-robust earnings expectations, a broadening of AI beneficiaries, fiscal support, increased capital spending, deregulation, rising mergers-and-acquisitions activity, and easing trade tensions. With the risks reasonably balanced and momentum still intact, the path of least resistance for the equity market may continue to be higher for now. The question is, can it reach double digits for the four-peat?

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio recorded a single-digit gain for the quarter and trailed its blended benchmark, comprising a 14.00% weighting in the Russell 3000 Index, a 6.00% weighting in the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (net), a 40.00% weighting in the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index and a 40.00% weighting in the Bloomberg 1-5 Year TIPS Index. Disciplined Value International Fund (John Hancock) was a noteworthy detractor for the quarter. The fund's lower allocation to value stocks and higher allocation to growth versus the MSCI EAFE Value Index was a headwind in a quarter where international value equities strongly outperformed growth names. Additionally, the portfolio's exposure to a hedged equity strategy detracted, as stock indexes moved higher, closing out a strong year. Conversely, Spectrum Income (T. Rowe Price) contributed modestly on a relative basis, largely due to our out-of-benchmark exposure to emerging-market bonds (both dollar-denominated and local currency). International Growth Fund (John Hancock) also contributed, aided by security selection in the financials sector. A tactical overweight to international value equities further contributed, given the relatively strong performance of that group.
